original_abstract:
The safe sequestration of radiological iodine-129 is a critical challenge for the expansion of sustainable nuclear energy and the management of nuclear waste. While silver-functionalized mordenite (MOR) is an established benchmark for iodine capture, its performance and molecular-level interactions within the complex, multi-component gas streams typical of nuclear fuel reprocessing—particularly concerning organic iodides like methyl iodide (CH3I)—remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigate the structure and distribution of iodine captured from CH3I-bearing streams by reduced silver mordenite (Ag°-MOR) using a suite of advanced characterization techniques, including synchrotron X-ray pair distribution function (PDF) analysis, Rietveld refinement, infrared spectroscopy, and thermogravimetric analysis coupled with mass spectrometry (TGA-MS).

Our results demonstrate a dual catalytic and sorptive mechanism: the Ag°-MOR framework facilitates the catalytic cleavage of CH3I at 150 °C, leading to the formation of surface methoxy species (SMS) at zeolite acid sites. The released iodine is chemically confined within the MOR pores as subnanometer AgI clusters and larger 6 nm nanoparticles, while the organic moieties undergo further reaction to form methanol and dimethyl ether, which are subsequently detected in the effluent. Structural analysis suggests that pore confinement stabilizes the AgI phase, preventing loss even at temperatures exceeding the bulk melting point. Additionally, we observe the competitive adsorption of NOx species, which influences the thermal stability of the captured iodine. These findings provide a molecular-level blueprint for optimizing zeolitic sorbents intended for the long-term immobilization of volatile radionuclides in complex industrial environments.
humanized_abstract:
Radiological iodine-129 is one of the major challenges for the development of advanced nuclear fuel cycles for sustainable nuclear energy and for nuclear waste disposal. Although silver-functionalized mordenite (MOR) is a benchmark for iodine removal, the behavior of this material and its molecular interactions in the complex multi-component gas mixtures that are found in nuclear fuel reprocessing facilities (NFRFs) for organic iodides such as methyl iodide (CH3I) are poorly understood. This study investigated the structure and distribution of iodine species extracted from CH3I contaminated gas mixtures using reduced silver mordenite (Ag°-MOR) and the effects thereon were elucidated using a range of complementary analytical techniques, namely, synchrotron X-ray pair distribution function (PDF) analysis and Rietveld refinement, infrared spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis coupled with mass spectrometry (TGA-MS).

Here we show that these materials can act not only as sorbents but also as catalysts for the dissociation of methyl iodide (CH3I) and hence that their catalytic/sorptive activity is a “dual function” mode. The catalyst is activated at 150 °C in air over the Ag°-MOR to give a catalyst product containing surface methoxy species (SMS) on the acid sites of the zeolite. CH4 and DME are the products of the further reactions occurring to the organic products (which are desorbed and identified in the effluent). The iodine is chemically held as subnanometer AgI clusters within the pores of the zeolite, as well as as larger 6 nm nanoparticles, the latter persisting even at temperatures well above the bulk melting point. Additionally, the structure shows that the NOx species adsorbed on the material affect its thermal stability and hence the behavior of the iodine held as AgI in the zeolite cavities. Hence, we have a structural model of zeolites that could be used for design of sorbents for immobilization of volatile radionuclides in different industrial processes for a longer time.
